docs.text: 👋 Hey there! Purpose This website documents my hands-on IT lab environment, built to simulate the infrastructure of a small enterprise network. Each project is interconnected as part of one larger environment, including Active Directory, domain-joined workstations, Group Policy, file services, network segmentation, permissions management, and future infrastructure services. The goal of this lab is to move beyond isolated tutorials and build a realistic environment where each technology supports the others. Through this project, I am developing practical experience in system administration, networking, identity management, troubleshooting, and technical documentation. Another Note! As a lifelong gamer, I wanted this lab to feel like more than just a collection of servers, users, and network diagrams. The naming conventions throughout the environment are inspired by the worlds, characters, and themes found in Hideo Kojima’s games. This gives the lab a bit of personality while still keeping the structure realistic and professional. Servers, workstations, users, groups, and other resources follow a consistent naming approach, but with references that make the environment more memorable and enjoyable to build. Think of it as a small enterprise network with a little tactical espionage flair. ~ Dylan Phan

docs.text: Networking Overview Server Network The server network in this lab will be used to house the various servers that will host critical domain resources and services. Server Role IP Address Kojima-DC01 Domain Controller / DNS 10.10.10.10 Kojima-FS01 File Server 10.10.10.20 Kojima-APPS01 Ubuntu server that will be used to serve Docker container applications to other machines on the network. 10.10.10.30 Workstation Network This network will house the Windows 11 clients that will be accessing services and resources from the server network Server Role IP Address Kojima-WS01 (Admin Machine) This machine will be used by a "IT Administrator." They should have the ability to create and manage domain resources and services DHCP Kojima-WS02 (Standard Machine) This is for a standard enterprise user that will not have administrator privileges DHCP
docs.title: Network Overview

docs.title: Workstation Network

docs.text: OpenWRT Virtual Machine Setup OpenWrt acts as the lab’s virtual router and network gateway. It provides routing, firewall control, DHCP services, and traffic separation between the server and workstation subnets, helping the environment behave more like a real small enterprise network. Preparing OpenWRT .vmdk OpenWrt is distributed as an .img disk image because it is primarily designed to be written directly to physical networking hardware. VMware Workstation does not natively support the .img format, so the image first needed to be converted into a VMware-compatible .vmdk virtual disk. After reviewing the available conversion methods, I chose to use the QEMU utilities within WSL 2 to convert the OpenWrt image into the required .vmdk format. Installing Qemu Tools on Ubuntu WSL 2.0 > sudo apt update > sudo apt install qemu-utils -y ### Creating Directory in my home folder for the files > mkdir openwrt Converting .img to .vmdk > qemu-img convert -f raw -O vmdk openwrt.img openwrt.vmdk > ls ### openwrt.img openwrt.vmdk VMware Workstation Settings To prepare the environment for OpenWrt, I first configured VMware’s virtual networks to act as the backbone of the lab. These networks allow the router, servers, and workstations to communicate while keeping each subnet properly separated. VMware Virtual Networks AD-SERVERNET — 10.10.10.0/24 Host-only VMware network Used by domain controllers, file servers, and other infrastructure servers VMware DHCP disabled IP addressing and network services are managed through OpenWrt AD-WORKSTATION — 10.10.20.0/24 Host-only VMware network Used by domain-joined workstations and client devices VMware DHCP disabled OpenWrt provides DHCP leases and routing for connected workstations WAN Connected to VMware’s NAT-enabled network DHCP enabled Allows the OpenWrt WAN interface to automatically receive an IP address Provides internet access to the internal server and workstation networks through OpenWrt OpenWRT Virtual Machine Settings Hard Disk: With the converted .vmdk file, we can attach the boot drive to the virtual hard disk image to boot into Openwrt. Network Adapter: Connected to VMware’s NAT-enabled network. This interface receives an IP address through DHCP and provides the OpenWrt router with internet access. Network Adapter 2: Connected to the AD-SERVERNET host-only network. This interface serves as the default gateway for domain controllers, file servers, and other infrastructure servers on the 10.10.10.0/24 subnet. Network Adapter 3: Connected to the AD-WORKSTATION host-only network. This interface provides routing and DHCP services for domain-joined workstations on the 10.10.20.0/24 subnet.
docs.title: OpenWRT VM

docs.title: OpenWRT Virtual Machine Settings

docs.text: OpenWRT Configuration Interface VMware Network Gateway eth0 VMware NAT DHCP Client eth1 AD-SERVERNET 10.10.10.1/24 eth2 AD-WORKSTATION 10.10.20.1/24 Verifying Interfaces ip link WAN Configuration Configuring WAN interface: uci set network.wan='interface' uci set network.wan.device='eth0' uci set network.wan.proto='dhcp' By default, OpenWRT assigned eth0 to the br-lan bridge and used it as the initial LAN interface. Because this adapter was intended to provide upstream internet connectivity, it needed to be removed from the LAN bridge and configured as a DHCP-based WAN interface. Removing eth0 from br-lan: uci del_list network.@device[0].ports='eth0' ### Commiting Changes and restarting network services uci commit network /etc/init.d/network restart ### Verifying br-lan has been removed ip addr Verifying eth0 is receiving IP Address: Verifying External Connectivity Server and Workstation Network Server - eth1 uci set network.lan.device='eth1' uci set network.lan.proto='static' uci set network.lan.ipaddr='10.10.10.1' uci set network.lan.netmask='255.255.255.0' Workstation - eth2 uci set network.workstation='interface' uci set network.workstation.device='eth2' uci set network.workstation.proto='static' uci set network.workstation.ipaddr='10.10.20.1' uci set network.workstation.netmask='255.255.255.0' Commit Changes uci commit network /etc/init.d/network restart Confirm Changes ip addr
docs.title: OpenWRT Config

docs.title: Server and Workstation Network

docs.text: OpenWRT Web Gui Note : I happened to have a Kali Linux Virtual Machine setup on my VMware Workstation, so I am using that to configure OpenWRT via the web GUI. Virtual Machine Network Settings First I attached the virtual adapter for the virtual machine to the AD-SERVERNET network then I configured the static IP for the machine inside the OS. After that, I did a simple ping test to make sure the virtual machine could reach the gateway (10.10.10.1) Accessing Web GUI IP Address: 10.10.10.1 To access the web gui, I used firefox and entered the gateway address above. By default, there is no password set on OpenWRT but that can be set upon initial login. Configuring Interfaces and DHCP Lan Interface -> Edit -> DHCP Server Disabling DHCP on server network (LAN Interface) Enabling DHCP on workstation network (workstation interface) Workstation Interface -> Edit -> DHCP Server Configuring these settings will allow our workstation network to give hosts IP address automatically with a range from 10.10.20.100 - 199 Assigning Workstation Interface to firewall zone Assigning the interface to the lan zone allowed DHCP traffic from the workstation network to reach OpenWrt. Verifying Addressing on both Networks Note: For context, I had a Fedora Workstation Virtual machine that I had to test DHCP connectivity. ip addr ### Used to test DNS ping google.com Kali VM (Server Network): Fedora VM (Workstation Network):
docs.title: OpenWRT Web GUI

docs.text: Domain Controller Setup Hostname: Kojima-DC01 IP Address: 10.10.10.10 Windows Version: Windows Server 2022 (Desktop Experience) Kojima-DC01 serves as the central domain controller for the lab, hosting Active Directory Domain Services (AD DS). It provides centralized authentication and allows domain users, computers, security groups, and other network resources to be managed from a single location. Virtual Machine Settings To keep the lab lightweight while maintaining reliable performance, the domain controller’s virtual hardware is configured with only the resources necessary to support its core services. Processors: 2 Cores Memory: 2 Gigabytes Hard Disk: 60 Gigabytes Network Adapter: AD-SERVERNET IPv4 Address Configuration Network & Internet Settings -> Change Adapter options -> Ethernet0 -> Properties -> Internet Protocol Version 4 IP Address: 10.10.10.10 Subnet Mask: 255.255.255.0 Default Gateway: 10.10.10.1 Preferred DNS Server: 10.10.10.10 For installing initial Windows Updates, the preferred DNS will point to the gateway but once DNS is configured to the DC then it will change to 10.10.10.10 Installing Active Directory Domain Services (AD DS) Validating Settings Before installing Active Directory Domain Services, I verified the server's hostname and network configuration using: hostname ipconfig /all Installing AD DS Using Server Manager, I installed the Active Directory Domain Services server role. Promoting server to Domain Controller Setting Configuration Server KOJIMA-DC01 Domain kojima.test Deployment Type New Forest DNS Server Installed Automatically Global Catalog Enabled Read-Only Domain Controller Disabled DNS was installed alongside Active Directory because domain clients rely on DNS service records to locate domain controllers and other domain services. The Global Catalog option was also enabled because this server would be the first and primary domain controller in the environment. Validation I opened Active Directory Users and Computers and confirmed that the kojima.test domain and its default containers were available I also confirmed that DNS contained the forward lookup zone and service records created for the domain. Get-ADDomain KOJIMA-DC01 was successfully promoted as the first domain controller and DNS server for the kojima.test forest. With the domain services operational, I could begin designing the organizational unit structure, creating users and security groups, and joining the first workstation to the domain.
docs.title: 🖥️ Domain Controller

docs.title: Validation

docs.text: Organizational Unit Design Before creating users, security groups, or administrative permissions, the first step was designing an Organizational Unit structure for the kojima.test domain. Getting this right upfront matters — the OU structure determines how Group Policy is applied, how delegation is scoped, and how cleanly the environment scales as new users, machines, and roles are added. The goal was to create a structure that would support: Clear separation between standard and privileged accounts Role-based access control via the AGDLP model Delegated Active Directory administration without requiring Domain Admin rights for routine tasks Targeted Group Policy application scoped to the right objects Department-specific workstation management Future expansion without structural rework Final Design The completed structure separates identities, computers, roles, and resource-access groups into five distinct top-level Organizational Units. Top Level OUs: OU Purpose Kojima Users Contains standard employee accounts Kojima Admins Contains dedicated privileged accounts Kojima Computers Contains domain-joined workstations and servers Kojima Roles Contains Global Security Groups representing job roles and administrative task permissions Kojima Resources Contains Domain Local Groups representing delegated permissions and resource access Identity OUs The identity OUs separate standard employee accounts from privileged administrative accounts. Kojima ├── Kojima Users │ ├── IT (Standard IT user accounts) │ ├── Development (Standard development user accounts) │ └── Operations (Standard operations user accounts) │ └── Kojima Admins ├── Domain Admins (Domain-level administrative accounts) ├── Server Admins (Server administrative accounts) └── Workstation Admins (Workstation administrative accounts) Standard and privileged accounts are stored separately so they can be managed independently. Administrative accounts receive stricter Group Policies, logon restrictions, auditing requirements, and privileged-access controls without those settings affecting standard user accounts. Each privileged account represents a specific administrative scope. A single person may hold multiple privileged accounts — one per scope — rather than a single elevated account used for all tasks. This enforces least privilege at the identity level. Computer OUs The computer OUs separate member servers from end-user workstations. Kojima Computers ├── Servers (Domain-joined member servers) ├── Staging (Newly joined machines pending OU assignment) └── Workstations (Domain-joined user endpoints) ├── IT (IT-assigned workstations) ├── Development (Developer workstations) └── Operations (Operations workstations) Servers and workstations are stored separately because they require different security baselines, administrative controls, and Group Policy settings. The Staging OU holds newly domain-joined machines before they are moved to their target OU. This prevents unvetted machines from immediately receiving production GPOs and gives administrators a window to configure and validate each machine before it enters the managed environment. The workstation structure is divided by department so that policies can be applied only where they are needed. For example: IT workstations may receive administrative utilities and support tooling. Development workstations may receive approved local administrator access and development tooling. Operations workstations may receive a more restrictive standard-user configuration. Roles OUs The Roles OUs contain Global Security Groups. These groups never hold permissions directly — they control membership. Permissions flow through the Resource OUs via the AGDLP chain described in the Task Groups section. There are three distinct sub-OUs under Kojima Roles , each serving a different purpose: Kojima Roles ├── Admin Roles (Scoped privileged identity groups) │ ├── Kojima-GG-IT-Role-SysAdmin-WS │ ├── Kojima-GG-IT-Role-SysAdmin-Server │ └── Kojima-GG-IT-Role-SysAdmin-Domain │ ├── Task Roles (Administrative task permission groups) │ ├── Kojima-GG-User-Management │ ├── Kojima-GG-Computer-Management │ ├── Kojima-GG-GPO-Management │ ├── Kojima-GG-Group-Management │ └── Kojima-GG-OU-Management │ └── Department Roles (Standard organizational membership groups) ├── IT │ └── Kojima-GG-IT-Staff ├── Development │ └── Kojima-GG-Development-Staff └── Operations └── Kojima-GG-Operations-Staff Admin Roles represent elevated administrative identities scoped by account tier. A Sr. Systems Engineer holds three separate Role GGs — one for workstation-level access, one for server-level access, and one for domain-level access. This ensures each privileged account carries only the permissions its scope requires. Task Roles represent specific administrative functions within Active Directory — managing users, computers, GPOs, groups, and OUs. No user or admin account is a direct member of a Task GG. Instead, Admin Role GGs are nested into the appropriate Task GGs, and Task GGs are nested into Domain Local permission groups. This compositional model means adding a new role requires only updating group membership, not redefining permissions. Department Roles represent standard organizational membership. A standard user account belongs to exactly one Department Role GG corresponding to their team. These groups are used for email distribution, resource access scoping, and future application role assignments. Resource OUs The Resource OUs contain Domain Local Security Groups. These groups hold the actual delegated ACLs and share/NTFS permissions. Nothing is ever assigned directly to a user or a Global Group — access always flows through a Domain Local group. Kojima Resources ├── Domain Access (Delegated Active Directory permissions) │ ├── Kojima-DL-Users-Manage │ ├── Kojima-DL-Computers-Manage │ ├── Kojima-DL-GPO-Manage │ ├── Kojima-DL-Groups-Manage │ └── Kojima-DL-OUs-Manage │ ├── Computer Access (Workstation and server management access) │ └── Kojima-DL-Workstations-LocalAdmin │ └── File Shares (NTFS and share-permission groups) └── Kojima-DL-FS01-IT-Modify Domain Local groups are scoped to a single permission target — one OU, one share, one resource. This keeps the blast radius of any delegation small and auditable. When access needs to be revoked, removing a Global Group from a Domain Local group is sufficient — no ACL editing required.
docs.title: OU Design

docs.title: Resource OUs

docs.text: Tiered Admin Model In many environments, administrators operate with a single elevated account used for everything — checking email, browsing the web, resetting passwords, and making domain-wide changes. This is a significant security risk. If that account is compromised, an attacker immediately has access to everything that account can touch. The Kojima lab addresses this with a tiered admin model. Each person is assigned multiple accounts, each scoped to a specific level of access. Elevated accounts are only used when the task requires that level of elevation — never for routine work. The Problem With a Single Admin Account A single admin account creates several compounding risks: Blast radius — a compromised credential exposes every system that account can reach No audit clarity — logs show the same account performing both routine and privileged actions, making forensic analysis harder GPO targeting — applying stricter security policies to privileged accounts is difficult when standard and admin work happen from the same identity Least privilege violations — domain-wide permissions are active even when the task only requires local workstation access A tiered model eliminates these problems by ensuring no single account carries more access than its specific purpose requires. The Four Account Tiers Each person in the Kojima domain is provisioned with up to four accounts depending on their role. Each account lives in a dedicated OU and carries only the permissions its scope demands. Tier Account Format OU Purpose Standard firstname.lastname Kojima Users Day-to-day work — email, files, applications. No elevated permissions. Workstation Admin lastname-ws Kojima Admins/Workstation Admins Local administrator rights on domain-joined workstations only. Server Admin lastname-sa Kojima Admins/Server Admins Administrative access to member servers. No domain-level AD permissions. Domain Admin lastname-da Kojima Admins/Domain Admins Domain-wide Active Directory tasks — managing users, groups, GPOs, and OUs. The standard account is used for all routine work. Elevated accounts are used only when the task explicitly requires that scope. A domain admin account should never be used to browse the web. A workstation admin account should never be used to modify AD objects. How Tiers Connect to Permissions — AGDLP Permissions in the Kojima domain are never assigned directly to a user account. Instead they flow through a four-layer chain known as AGDLP: A — Account (the user or admin account) G — Global Group (the Role GG representing who the account is) DL — Domain Local Group (the Permission group holding the actual ACL) P — Permission (the delegated right or resource access) The Task GG layer sits between G and DL as a compositional middle tier — it groups related permissions so that multiple Role GGs can share the same task permissions without duplicating delegations. The full chain looks like this: Domain Admin account example: lastname-da └── Kojima-GG-IT-Role-SysAdmin-Domain (Role GG — who this account is) └── Kojima-GG-User-Management (Task GG — what task is permitted) │ └── Kojima-DL-Users-Manage (DL — holds the delegated ACL) │ └── [Delegated permission on Kojima Users OU] │ └── Kojima-GG-Computer-Management │ └── Kojima-DL-Computers-Manage │ └── [Delegated permission on Kojima Computers OU] │ └── Kojima-GG-GPO-Management │ └── Kojima-DL-GPO-Manage │ └── [Delegated permission in GPMC] │ └── Kojima-GG-Group-Management │ └── Kojima-DL-Groups-Manage │ └── [Delegated permission on group objects] │ └── Kojima-GG-OU-Management └── Kojima-DL-OUs-Manage └── [Delegated permission on OU objects] Workstation Admin account example: lastname-ws └── Kojima-GG-IT-Role-SysAdmin-WS (Role GG — scoped to workstations only) └── Kojima-GG-Computer-Management (Task GG — computer management only) └── Kojima-DL-Computers-Manage └── [Delegated permission on Kojima Workstations OU] The workstation admin account nests into a single Task GG. The domain admin account nests into all five. The account scope determines which Role GG it belongs to, and the Role GG determines which Task GGs it feeds into. Permissions are defined once and composed — never duplicated. Why This Model Scales When a new role is added — such as a HelpDesk analyst — only two things need to happen: Create a Role GG for that title ( Kojima-GG-IT-Role-HelpDesk ) Nest it into the appropriate Task GGs (e.g. Kojima-GG-User-Management only) No new delegations. No new DL groups. No ACL editing. The permission infrastructure already exists — the new role simply plugs into it. This is the core advantage of the AGDLP model over direct permission assignment. The more roles and resources the environment grows to include, the more this separation pays off.
docs.title: Admin Model

docs.title: Why This Model Scales

docs.text: User Accounts To begin the account-provisioning process, the lab will focus on a single user: Hal Emmerich. Within the Metal Gear universe, Hal Emmerich is best known as a highly skilled engineer and technology specialist. In the context of this lab, he will serve as a Senior Systems Engineer within the IT department. Because this role requires access to multiple systems and administrative functions, Hal will be assigned a standard user account for everyday work alongside separate privileged accounts for workstation, server, and domain-level administration. This approach supports role-based access control while maintaining a clear separation between routine tasks and elevated responsibilities. Account: Username OU Description hal.emmerich Kojima Users/IT Used for day to day activities (web browsing, working on files, etc.) hemmerich-ws Kojima Admins/Workstation Admins Used for local administrator actions on workstations hemmerich-sa Kojima Admins/Server Admins Used for server administrative activities hemmerich-da Kojima Admins/Domain Admins Used for domain administrative actions (creating users, resetting passwords, etc.) Account Creation Hal's various accounts were made in Powershell ISE. Hre is a snippet of how one of those accounts can be created: Powershell: Import-Module ActiveDirectory $Password = Read-Host "Enter a temporary password" -AsSecureString try { New-ADUser ` -Name "Hal Emmerich" ` -GivenName "Hal" ` -Surname "Emmerich" ` -DisplayName "Hal Emmerich" ` -SamAccountName "hal.emmerich" ` -UserPrincipalName "hal.emmerich@kojima.test" ` -Title "Systems Engineer" ` -Department "IT" ` -Description "Standard user account for Hal Emmerich" ` -Path "OU=IT,OU=Kojima Users,OU=Kojima,DC=kojima,DC=test" ` -AccountPassword $Password ` -ChangePasswordAtLogon $true ` -Enabled $true ` -ErrorAction Stop Write-Host "User Created." -ForegroundColor Green } catch { Write-Host "User failed to be created" -ForegroundColor Red } Powershell ISE: Creating Admin Accounts via Powershell: The standard account was placed within the IT department under the Kojima Users OU, while the administrative accounts were separated into dedicated OUs beneath Kojima Admins . At this stage, the accounts have only been created and organized. Their permissions will be assigned later through security groups, Group Policy, and delegated Active Directory permissions. Validating Account Creation Powershell Command: Get-ADUser -Filter { SamAccountName -eq "hal.emmerich" -or SamAccountName -like "hemmerich-*" } -Properties Description | Select-Object ` Name, SamAccountName, Description, @{Name="OU"; Expression={ if ($_.DistinguishedName -match ',OU=([^,]+)') { $Matches[1] } }} | Sort-Object SamAccountName | Format-Table -AutoSize -Wrap Powershell Command:
docs.title: User Accounts

docs.text: Hal's various accounts were made in Powershell ISE. Hre is a snippet of how one of those accounts can be created: Powershell: Import-Module ActiveDirectory $Password = Read-Host "Enter a temporary password" -AsSecureString try { New-ADUser ` -Name "Hal Emmerich" ` -GivenName "Hal" ` -Surname "Emmerich" ` -DisplayName "Hal Emmerich" ` -SamAccountName "hal.emmerich" ` -UserPrincipalName "hal.emmerich@kojima.test" ` -Title "Systems Engineer" ` -Department "IT" ` -Description "Standard user account for Hal Emmerich" ` -Path "OU=IT,OU=Kojima Users,OU=Kojima,DC=kojima,DC=test" ` -AccountPassword $Password ` -ChangePasswordAtLogon $true ` -Enabled $true ` -ErrorAction Stop Write-Host "User Created." -ForegroundColor Green } catch { Write-Host "User failed to be created" -ForegroundColor Red } Powershell ISE: Creating Admin Accounts via Powershell: The standard account was placed within the IT department under the Kojima Users OU, while the administrative accounts were separated into dedicated OUs beneath Kojima Admins . At this stage, the accounts have only been created and organized. Their permissions will be assigned later through security groups, Group Policy, and delegated Active Directory permissions.

docs.title: Validating Account Creation

docs.text: Task Groups Task Groups and Delegations Task Group Delegation Description Kojima-GG-Task-User-Management Kojima-DL-Users-Manage User management Kojima-GG-Task-Computer-Management Kojima-DL-Computers-Manage Computer object management Kojima-GG-Task-GPO-Management Kojima-DL-GPOs-Manage Group Policy Object Management Kojima-GG-Task-Group-Management Kojima-DL-Groups-Manage Group object management Kojima-GG-Task-OU-Management Kojima-DL-OUs-Manage OU management Creating Task Global Groups Using Powershell to create Global Groups: Import-Module ActiveDirectory $GroupPath = "OU=Task Roles,OU=Kojima Roles,OU=Kojima,DC=kojima,DC=test" try { New-ADGroup ` -Name "Kojima-GG-Task-User-Managment" ` -SamAccountName "Kojima-GG-Task-User-Managment" ` -GroupCategory Security ` -GroupScope Global ` -Path $GroupPath ` -Description "Task group for user management" ` -ErrorAction Stop ... Write-Host "Global Task groups created successfully." -ForegroundColor Green } catch { Write-Host "Group creation failed." -ForegroundColor Red Write-Host $_.Exception.Message -ForegroundColor Yellow } Validating global task group creation: Creating all Domain Local Groups Using Powershell to create Domain Local groups: Import-Module ActiveDirectory $GroupPath = "OU=Domain Access,OU=Kojima Resources,OU=Kojima,DC=kojima,DC=test" try { New-ADGroup ` -Name "Kojima-DL-Users-Manage" ` -SamAccountName "Kojima-DL-Users-Manage" ` -GroupCategory Security ` -GroupScope DomainLocal ` -Path $GroupPath ` -Description "Domain local group for managing users" ` -ErrorAction Stop ... Write-Host "Global Task groups created successfully." -ForegroundColor Green } catch { Write-Host "Group creation failed." -ForegroundColor Red Write-Host $_.Exception.Message -ForegroundColor Yellow } Nesting Groups Using Powershell to nest groups Import-Module ActiveDirectory try { Add-ADGroupMember ` -Identity "Kojima-DL-Users-Manage" ` -Members "Kojima-GG-Task-User-Management" ... Write-Host "Groups have been nested successfully." -ForegroundColor Green } catch { Write-Host "Group nesting failed." -ForegroundColor Red Write-Host $_.Exception.Message -ForegroundColor Yellow } Powershell ISE: Validating via Powershell: $DLGroups = @( "Kojima-DL-Users-Manage", "Kojima-DL-Computers-Manage", "Kojima-DL-GPOs-Manage", "Kojima-DL-Groups-Manage", "Kojima-DL-OUs-Manage" ) foreach ($group in $DLGroups) { Write-Host "`nMembers of $group" -ForegroundColor Cyan Get-ADGroupMember -Identity $group | Select-Object Name, objectClass, SamAccountName | Format-Table -AutoSize } Delegating Permissions User Management ADUC -> Kojima Users -> Delegate Control -> Add Kojima-DL-Users-Manage Computer Management ADUC -> Kojima Computers -> Delegate Control -> Create a custom task to delegate Read Write Read all properties Write all properties Create computer objects Delete computer objects GPO Management GPMC -> kojima.test -> Group Policy Objects -> Delegation -> Kojima-DL-GPOs-Manage GPMC -> kojima.test -> Delegation -> Add Kojima-DL-GPOs-Manage Group Management ADUC -> Kojima -> Delegate Control -> Add Kojima-DL-Groups-Manage -> Create a custom task to delegate Create, delete, and manage groups Modify the memberships of a group OU Management Powershell: $OU = "OU=Kojima,DC=kojima,DC=test" $Group = "KOJIMA\Kojima-DL-OUs-Manage" dsacls $OU /G "$($Group):CCDC;organizationalUnit" /I:S dsacls $OU /G "$($Group):WP;;" /I:S Validation: dsacls "OU=Kojima,DC=kojima,DC=test" | Where-Object { $_ -like "*OUs-Manage*" } dsacls "OU=Kojima,DC=kojima,DC=test" | Where-Object { $_ -like "*OUs-Manage*" -or $_ -like "*organizationalUnit*" -or $_ -like "*CREATE*" -or $_ -like "*DELETE*" -or $_ -like "*WRITE*" } | Select-Object -First 20
docs.title: Task Groups

docs.text: Task Groups and Delegations Task Group Delegation Description Kojima-GG-Task-User-Management Kojima-DL-Users-Manage User management Kojima-GG-Task-Computer-Management Kojima-DL-Computers-Manage Computer object management Kojima-GG-Task-GPO-Management Kojima-DL-GPOs-Manage Group Policy Object Management Kojima-GG-Task-Group-Management Kojima-DL-Groups-Manage Group object management Kojima-GG-Task-OU-Management Kojima-DL-OUs-Manage OU management

docs.text: Role Groups Role Groups Role Description Kojima-GG-Role-SysAdmin-DA Domain access role for those with SysAdmin title or above Kojima-GG-Role-SysAdmin-WS Workstation access role for those with SysAdmin title or above Kojima-GG-Role-SysAdmin-SA Server access role for those with SysAdmin title or above Creating Role Groups via PowerShell: Import-Module ActiveDirectory $GroupPath = "OU=Admin Roles,OU=Kojima Roles,OU=Kojima,DC=kojima,DC=test" try { New-ADGroup ` -Name "Kojima-GG-Role-SysAdmin-DA" ` -SamAccountName "Kojima-GG-Role-SysAdmin-DA" ` -GroupCategory Security ` -GroupScope Global ` -Path $GroupPath ` -Description "Role Group for System Administrator Domain Access" ` -ErrorAction Stop Write-Host "Global Role groups created successfully." -ForegroundColor Green } catch { Write-Host "Group creation failed." -ForegroundColor Red Write-Host $_.Exception.Message -ForegroundColor Yellow } Validating that Role Groups were created:

docs.text: Domain Administrator Account Due to Hal's title of 'Systems Engineer' he will require a high level of access and control of domain resources. Account : hemmerich-da Permissions: Manage User accounts (create, delete, modify, membership, reset passwords) Group Management (Create, delete, modify) OU management (Create, delete, modify) GPO management (Create, delete, modify) Computer Management (Create, delete, modify) Applying task roles to Domain Administrator Role Import-Module ActiveDirectory try { Add-ADGroupMember ` -Identity "Kojima-GG-User-Management" ` -Members "Kojima-GG-Role-SysAdmin-DA" Add-ADGroupMember ` -Identity "Kojima-GG-Computer-Management" ` -Members "Kojima-GG-Role-SysAdmin-DA" Add-ADGroupMember ` -Identity "Kojima-GG-GPO-Management" ` -Members "Kojima-GG-Role-SysAdmin-DA" Add-ADGroupMember ` -Identity "Kojima-GG-Group-Management" ` -Members "Kojima-GG-Role-SysAdmin-DA" Add-ADGroupMember ` -Identity "Kojima-GG-OU-Management" ` -Members "Kojima-GG-Role-SysAdmin-DA" Write-Host "Membership applied successfully" -ForegroundColor Green } catch { Write-Host "Membership change failed." -ForegroundColor Red Write-Host $_.Exception.Message -ForegroundColor Yellow } Validating that task roles were added: Applying Role to Domain Administrator Account Applying membership via PowerShell Import-Module ActiveDirectory try { Add-ADGroupMember ` -Identity "Kojima-GG-Role-SysAdmin-DA" ` -Members "hemmerich-da" Write-Host "Membership applied successfully" -ForegroundColor Green } catch { Write-Host "Membership change failed." -ForegroundColor Red Write-Host $_.Exception.Message -ForegroundColor Yellow } Validating membership

docs.text: File Server Setup Hostname: Kojima-FS01 IP Address: 10.10.10.20 Windows Version: Windows Server 2022 (Desktop Experience) Virtual Machine Settings To keep the lab lightweight while maintaining reliable performance, the domain controller’s virtual hardware is configured with only the resources necessary to support its core services. Processors: 2 Cores Memory: 2 Gigabytes Hard Disk: 60 Gigabytes Network Adapter: AD-SERVERNET Extra Virtual Hard Disk: 60 GB IPv4 Address Configuration Network & Internet Settings -> Change Adapter options -> Ethernet0 -> Properties -> Internet Protocol Version 4 IP Address: 10.10.10.20 Subnet Mask: 255.255.255.0 Default Gateway: 10.10.10.1 Preferred DNS Server: 10.10.10.10 Joining kojima.test domain Validating Settings Before installing Active Directory Domain Services, I verified the server's hostname and network configuration using: hostname ipconfig /all Output: Joining Domain Server Manager -> Local Server -> Workgroup -> Change -> Domain: kojima.test
